On Thursday, the President said he would continue to deal with the deficit, including lowering the corporate tax rate to 9.3 percent as opposed to 1.5 percent, along with a 2.2 percent increase for low-income individuals without insurance. (White House) The Republican plan would reduce the federal budget deficit by about $5 trillion over the next decade, but it would reduce the federal deficit by about $14 trillion. Trump said Thursday that the White House would continue to deal with the deficit, although he has been unaware of the plan. On Thursday, Trump said he supports slashing the corporate tax rate by 25 percent, as opposed to a Democratic plan, but the House Democrat majority leader said there’s “no way forward” to the idea of slashing the rates. Representative Jim Issa (R-Utah), the ranking member of the House Financial Services Committee, said he was unaware of the Republican plan, although the House Judiciary Chairman would be unaware of Trump’s plan. “I think there’s no question of the economic impact, and it’s up to the Judiciary to go through the details,” House Financial Services Committee Vice Chairman Bob Busch said in a statement. “It’s impossible to overstate the economic impact of any of these figures, and it’s up to the House Judiciary to go through the details.” majority Leader Paul Ryan (R-Texas) said he doesn’t believe the Republican plan will reduce the federal budget deficit by several important measures, including a reduction in corporate income taxes and an additional trillion in new tax cuts. He said he understands the Republican plan would address the need to reduce the federal budget deficit. Ryan said the Republican plan would reduce the budget deficit by about $14 trillion over the next decade, but his office did not verify whether it would indeed reduce the budget deficit. The White House on Thursday unveiled another Republican revision to the Affordable Care Act, which calls for increasing the minimum wage rate for half or more of the rich, and lowering the coverage rate for individuals age 50 and over. BEIJING, June 5 (UPI) — An Islamic State militant group, formerly known as Isra, has launched a pair of HLS (Hiliconic Sár) anti-subspice missiles, Syria's government has said. An unidentified U.S. military source said that an air strike killed two people on Friday after one of the militants flew into the country. The missiles were launched from Isra's training base in north of the Syrian city of Raqqa, Syria's government said in a statement. The launch marks the first active missile to come into use, and is the first in a long series of missile launches carried out by Isra in the Middle East. The missiles, which are nearly 100 meters in length and weigh more than 200-gallons, are capable of traveling up to 10,000 miles at a speed of up to 6,000 meters per second. A military source said in a statement that the missile's anti-subspice capabilities help disrupt attacks as well as improve air defense effectiveness. "There is no way for [a] missile to function for such a long period without accompanied by a missile attached to it," that source reportedly said in a statement. (TC.O) will not enforce President Trump's travel ban for mentally ill Americans, a claim the U.S. Federal Trade Commission has denied. "Most Americans are likely to switch to high-waged housings at the end of a 10-year trend," said John Chafus, an associate professor of economics at the University Monte-Mlorn School of Economics. Chafus projected that the average rents to lower-cost housings over the next 10 years would fall by $35,000 a month for medium-cost housings, $10,000 a month for medium-cost housings and $14,000 a year for high-wage housings. While average rents to medium-cost housings increased from $35,000 a month to $14,000 a year, the average rents rose only by $18,000 a year, Chafus said. People transitioning to medium-cost housings may still have more work than all average Americans, but may nonetheless be less likely to find part-time work. One of the chief reasons that medium-cost housings are more likely to continue to grow is because many Americans may have difficulty transitioning to higher-wage housings, he said. Those changing to lower-cost housings may end up looking for jobs and looking for part-time work, he said. The tendency to switch to a higher-cost housing may be a result of factors related to the slowing economy, he said. "The recession has created scarce job opportunities for those with health problems, those with chronic illness, those suffering from addiction, and for those leaving the labor force," he said. The tendency to switch to higher-cost housings may be related to a combination of part-time job opportunities, poor education, low wages, and lack of care, he said. "High prices of food and entertainment have made people less well cared for — especially people who have been working all their adult lives — and have increased the risk of people losing work when they have been having the worst years of their lives," he said. The slowing economy has been a contributor to rising unemployment rates and the declining jobs of many part-time workers, he said. The share of Americans working part-time has risen by more than 40 percentage points in the past 10 years, he said. But the unemployment rate has remained steady, at its lowest level since 1989. More than two-thirds (39 percent) of Americans aged 60 years old or over said they are living in higher-cost housings, and almost a quarter of those aged 50 years younger said they are living in lower-cost ones. Nearly a tenth of Americans said they are living in medium-cost housings, a decline of seven percentage points since 2008. Those 60 years over are less likely to live in lower-cost housings than those aged 50 or above.
